flashcards.front: What is the primary purpose of enabling and disabling interrupts in a processor?
flashcards.back: To allow critical sections of code to execute atomically without being preempted by an interrupt service routine (ISR), ensuring data consistency and preventing race conditions.

flashcards.front: What is the typical name of the special processor instruction used to disable all interrupts?
flashcards.back: The instruction is often called Disable Interrupts (DI) or Clear Interrupt Flag (CLI). It sets a global interrupt mask bit in the processor's status register.

flashcards.front: What is the typical name of the instruction used to re-enable interrupts?
flashcards.back: The instruction is often called Enable Interrupts (EI) or Set Interrupt Flag (STI). It clears the global interrupt mask bit, allowing the processor to accept interrupt requests.

flashcards.front: Why is it considered bad practice to keep interrupts disabled for a long period of time?
flashcards.back: It increases interrupt latency, which is the delay between when an interrupt is requested and when it is serviced. This can lead to missed real-time deadlines or loss of input data.

A nested interrupt occurs when a higher-priority interrupt is allowed to preempt the execution of a lower-priority interrupt service routine (ISR). This requires careful management of the interrupt enable state.
